answers.intake_q2: What symptoms are you experiencing besides cough or difficulty breathing?
answers.intake_a2: runny nose, phelgm, sore throat, headache, fever, chills
answers.intake_q3: Any chest pain or worsening shortness of breath?
answers.intake_q4: Have you noticed any blood in your phlegm or experienced any confusion or severe weakness?
answers.intake_q5: Have you taken any medications or treatments for your symptoms so far?
answers.intake_q6: Do you have any history of lung disease, recent hospitalizations, or immune system problems?
answers.intake_q7: Any fever currently or in the past 24 hours?
answers.intake_q8: Have you experienced any difficulty breathing at rest or with minimal exertion?
answers.intake_q9: Have you noticed any swelling or pain in your legs, or any recent travel or immobilization?
answers.intake_q10: Have you had any recent exposure to anyone with confirmed or suspected COVID-19 or other respiratory infections?

gating_notes: renal disease status is yes/unknown (dose adjustment / avoid certain meds)

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 34-year-old female with a 5-day history of cough accompanied by runny nose, phlegm production, sore throat, headache, fever, and chills. She reports only mild chest pain and no significant shortness of breath at rest or with minimal exertion. There is no hemoptysis, confusion, or severe weakness. She has taken Dayquil for symptom relief. She denies any history of lung disease, recent hospitalizations, or immune system problems. She has had recent exposure to a coworker diagnosed with pneumonia. She has a known allergy to penicillin and a history of renal disease but no liver disease. Patient is not pregnant. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no vital signs or physical exam findings available.

A: 
Working diagnosis is community-acquired pneumonia given the constellation of fever, productive cough, chills, sore throat, headache, and known exposure to a confirmed pneumonia case. The mild chest pain and absence of severe respiratory distress or hemoptysis are consistent with a less severe presentation. 

Differential: 
1. Community-acquired pneumonia 
2. Acute bronchitis 
3. Viral upper respiratory infection 
4. Atypical pneumonia (e.g., Mycoplasma or viral etiology) 

P: 
 - Medication support:
   - Plan assessment:
     - Working impression based on telephone history. Management below is limited by telephone-only evaluation (no vitals/labs available during the call).
   - Rx candidates:
     - Supportive care — symptom relief and supportive management
   - Gating flags:
     - Pregnancy: no
     - Renal disease: yes
     - Liver disease: no
   - Return precautions:
     - Go to urgent care/ED now for severe or rapidly worsening symptoms, trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, fainting, or inability to keep fluids down.
     - Seek urgent in-person care for new high fever, severe pain, dehydration concerns, or any red-flag symptoms discussed during the call.
   - Testing:
     - If no improvement within 48–72 hours, worsening symptoms, or recurrent symptoms: seek in-person evaluation for appropriate testing (e.g., urinalysis/urine culture or other workup as indicated).
- First-line medication: Azithromycin oral, dosing and duration not specified in evidence; use with caution given renal disease and penicillin allergy (EVID_2). 
- Alternatives: 
 • Doxycycline oral, dosing and duration not specified; avoid if contraindicated in pregnancy (not applicable here) and consider renal function. 
 • Levofloxacin oral, dosing and duration not specified; use cautiously due to renal disease. 
- Medication safety considerations: Avoid penicillin and related beta-lactams due to allergy. Renal disease requires cautious use and possible dose adjustment of antibiotics. No pregnancy concerns. 
- Return precautions: Seek urgent care if worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, hemoptysis, confusion, severe weakness, or high fever persists despite treatment. 
- Testing: If no improvement within 48–72 hours, worsening symptoms, or recurrent symptoms: seek in-person evaluation for appropriate testing (e.g., urinalysis/urine culture or other workup as indicated).

evidence.title: Pneumonia | Pneumonia Symptoms | Signs of Pneumonia | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet:
e , liver disease , and kidney disease . 
What are the symptoms of pneumonia? 
The symptoms of pneumonia can range from mild to severe and include:
Fever 
Chills 
Cough, usually with phlegm (a type of thick mucus made in your lungs) 
Shortness of breath 
Chest pain when you breathe or cough 
Nausea and/or vomiting 
Diarrhea 
The symptoms can vary for different groups. Newborns and infants may not show any signs of the infection. Others may vomit and have a fever and cough. They might seem sick, with no energy, or be restless.
Older adults and people who have serious illnesses or weak immune systems may have fewer and milder symptoms. They may even have a lower-than-normal temperature. Older adults who have pneumonia sometimes may feel weak or suddenly get confused.
What other problems can pneumonia cause? 
Sometimes pneumonia can cause serious complications such as:
Bacteremia, which hap

Conditions A to Z
Pneumonia 
Pneumonia is inflammation of the lungs, usually caused by an infection. Most people get better in 2 to 4 weeks, but babies, older people, and people with heart or lung conditions are at risk of getting seriously ill and may need treatment in hospital. 
Symptoms of pneumonia 
Symptoms of pneumonia can start suddenly or gradually over a few days.
They include:
a cough – you may cough up yellow or green mucus (phlegm) shortness of breath a high temperature chest pain an aching body feeling very tired loss of appetite making wheezing noises when you breathe – babies may also make grunting noises feeling confused – this is common in older people 
Ur

evidence.title: Abatacept Injection: MedlinePlus Drug Information
evidence.snippet: t includes chronic bronchitis and emphysema); any disease that affects your nervous system, such as multiple sclerosis; hepatitis B (a virus that affects the liver); diabetes; high blood pressure; or any disease that affects your immune system, such as cancer, human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS), or severe combined immunodeficiency syndrome (SCID). Also tell your doctor if you have or have ever had tuberculosis (TB; a lung infection that may not cause symptoms for many years and that may spread to other parts of the body) or if you have been around someone who has or has had tuberculosis. Your doctor may give you a skin test to see whether you are infected with tuberculosis. Tell your doctor if you have ever had a positive skin test for tuberculosis in the past. If you are receiving abatacept to prevent graft versus host disease, tell your doctor

Tuberculosis (TB) 
Tuberculosis (TB) is an infection that usually affects the lungs. It can be treated with antibiotics but can be serious if not treated. There's a vaccine that helps protect some people who are at risk from TB. 
Symptoms of tuberculosis (TB) 
Symptoms of tuberculosis (TB) usually come on gradually.
Common symptoms include:
a cough that lasts more than 3 weeks – you may cough up mucus (phlegm) or mucus with blood in it feeling tired or exhausted a high temperature or night sweats loss of appetite weight loss feeling generally unwell Children may also have difficulty gaining weight or growing.
If TB has spread to another part of yo

evidence.title: Legionella Tests: MedlinePlus Medical Test
evidence.snippet:
e positive or negative, your provider may do other tests to confirm or rule out a diagnosis of Legionnaires' disease. These include:
Chest x-rays 
Gram Stain 
Acid Fast Bacillus (AFB) Tests 
Bacteria Culture 
Sputum Culture 
Respiratory Pathogens Panel 
If you have Legionnaires' disease, you may have symptoms such as fatigue and muscle aches for several months after treatment. If there's an outbreak of Legionnaires' disease in a building where you work or live, ask your provider if you need to do anything to reduce your risk of infection.
Legionnaires' disease got its name in 1976 after a group of people attending an American Legion convention became ill with pneumonia.
References 
American Lung Association [Internet]. Chicago: American Lung Association; c2025. Learn About Legionnaires' Disease; [updated 2024 Oct 30; cited 2025 Sep 9]; [about 2 screens]. Available from: https://www.lung.
